title: Route session-summary + L2 distillation through the LLM router (Qwen writes the memories)
current_state: The memory-writing LLM calls bypass the router: agent/session.py::generate_session_summary is called as generate_session_summary(self.groq, self.messages, ...) with the bare Groq client; L2 distillation similarly hardcodes Groq. So memory writes can't use Qwen, ignore the tier/TPD-budget/brownout logic in core/llm_router.py, and have their own failover story.
target_state: Both the session-end summary and L2 distillation take the router and call router.chat(..., tier='cheap'). With the hackathon tier ordering, that means Qwen-first on the deployed box and Groq locally, via one failover + budget-aware path. Narrative for the Track-1 submission: 'Qwen both writes and recalls the memories.' Output shapes (summary string / distilled fact list) are unchanged.
migration_plan: 1. Change generate_session_summary to accept the router (or an already-selected callable) instead of the Groq client; internally call router.chat(messages=..., system=..., tier='cheap', max_tokens=...).
2. Do the same for the L2 distillation call path in tools/tools_memory.py.
3. Update call sites in pi_agent.py (_generate_session_summary and the distillation trigger) to pass self.router.
4. Unit test: mock the router and assert the summary path calls router.chat with tier='cheap' (not the bare Groq client), and that the returned summary string is preserved.
5. python scripts/verify.py must PASS (test_session_exit_retention, test_persistence, and the memory contract tests included).
risk_notes: Keep the output contract identical — callers expect a plain summary string and a distilled-fact list; only the transport changes. Don't alter retention timing or the session-exit budget (T-112). This is independent of the retrieval tickets (T-290..T-293) and can be done in parallel. Locally (no QWEN_API_KEY) tier='cheap' still resolves to Groq/Cerebras, so behavior is unchanged off the deployed box.
